one_liner: Still the house wine of programming languages. Ages fine, just don't skip the vintage updates.
It's been dead for years. We're sorry.
Hot right now. Ask again in three years.
Aging well, like wine. Every generation of tools still needs to talk to a database.
The gold rush skill of the decade. Just keep panning for new techniques.
Never goes fully out of style; the threats just keep remodeling the house.
Whichever cloud you pick, the skill of picking clouds keeps paying off.
The cockroach of office software. Will outlive us all.
Half spreadsheet, half crystal ball. Demand keeps climbing.
Generative AI is eating the easy 80% of this job. The remaining 20% is where the real craft lives.
Digital transformation's favorite skill. Software still needs a human face.
GenAI drafts the first pass now. You're the editor, not the typist.
A durable skill in a world of shifting tools. PMI cert optional, backbone required.
The #1 riser of the last cycle. Machines still can't rally a team before a deadline.
Zero GenAI substitution potential. Some things stay human.
Analytical Thinking tops WEF's core-skills list for a reason: everyone needs it, nobody automates it away.
The one skill that's actually MORE important in growing jobs than declining ones.
Licensure-backed and physically hands-on. This one has a long shelf life.
A trade skill with real staying power, but automation nibbles at the edges.
WEF's own data says clerical roles are shrinking fastest. This one's on the clearance rack.
Manual precision skills are the only category with a true net decline. Sorry, fingers.
The channel changes, the nerve required doesn't.
Spreadsheets evolve, the underlying logic barely does.
Green transition is one of WEF's five named macrotrends. This skill rides that wave.
Environmental Stewardship posted the largest sector-specific gains in the WEF data.
AI can write the speech. It still can't deliver it with your timing.
Regulation moves slower than tech, but cybersecurity is dragging this one uphill.
